CURRENT AFFAIRS.

1t is 6nly in one’s early days—and not always then
—that one has a chance of observing many sides of
life. In the early days you go to all sorts of places,
Jou listen to many things, you are interested in many
things. As you grow mature and come to the years
of judgment you form opinions and tastes. 1f you

-are sensible you give up the society of fools, and if

you are foolish you keep without ear-shot of the wise.
Whatever way your character forms you go more or

_less into a groove, and life outside that groove becomes

foreign to you. These reflections are suggested by
the fact that we took a trip into foreign %{;rts recently
—we went to a pantomime in Dublin. e have long
since left those dull and characteristically Engish forms
of alleged entertainment behind us. 1t had been

years since we were at such a thing. - The imported.
pantomime is not in our world, and it was, as it were,.
going to a foreign land, to g to the last night’s pes-
formance of the pantomime at the *“ Gaiety ** Theatre-
There was not much gaiety there for us; it was all as
dull as ditch -water and as boring as a too-oft told tale,
yet the audience seemed to like that sort of thing.
There were two alleged comedians, and we suspect thaf:
a couple of ordinary seaside coons would at least be-
cleverer.  Four or five well-dressed, well-fed young
men behind us, probably students living in Dublin on
their papas’ allowances, were a distraction, and not-
an absolutely sober one; near the orchestra a couple
of gangs of silly men-boys indulged in a demonstration.
now and again; evidently to their vacuous minds the
dull performance was a thing of genius. Some of the
boxes W occupied by living fashion plates and-
Johnnies in evening dress ; and one of the ‘ humorous’”
songs at this display of English fun was, needless to
say, about high smelling cheese. . The minds that see:
ennial humour in that unsavory and disgusting sub-
ject are absolute puzzles to us. That particular Dublin
audience was, however, a characteristic part of what
we call the Dublin people, and Dublin, according to,
the geographies, is the capital of Ireland. Probably
the majority of the people of Ireland are no better in-
tellectually than that audience.

It is depressing from one point of view: from another
point of view it has its bright side. ~This class repre--
sent the portion of the people without any originality
or force; the pantomime and the dirty cheese song are
established facts in Anglicised Ireland, and these
people think them all right. The people who do things
and move things are comparatively small in number:
everywhere and every time; the respectable
class and other mere mobs cannot change
by virtue of any forcé within themselves,.
but a  vigorous ~and  comparatively  small
minority of the people resolved, in a reasonably:deter-
mined ‘way, to Irishise Ireland, have a good chance of
succeeding. The fight is really between minorities,
the majority will wag their tails after the stronger
team.  For the moment the majority troops: to an

English pantomime, one of the ‘‘ humorous '' songs

in which is about dirty cheese. Unreflecting people
are often staggered and appalled when they come face
to face with the Anglicised majority. But after all on
reflection, you come to the conclusion that the young
silly bloods who go to the imported pantomime, and
the female fashion plates and their Johnnies in evening
dress in the boxes, whose constitutions are too-weals
for thinking and who divide the world into what is-
‘“rotten ' and what is ‘‘ ripping,’’ are only pawns in:
the game. You could not, by an appeal to their
brains, as they do not possess any, alter their outlook on
Troland. The fight is not with them. When the tide-
flows in the Irish direction the ‘ ripping*’ Johnnies:
and the Jackeens will, in their own humble way, drift
with the current.

There is one thing about the Orangemen that we may
emphasise to our readers.
generation after generation, to bring them up m their
traditions. The traditions are obscurant, bigoted and

narrow, but it is not of the quality of the traditions we -

refer, but to the fact that they are brought up in them.

tions, are largely allowing the young generation ta

row-minded Orange anti-Catholic bigot is a finer type

The “programme-for next Sunday’s Orchestral Con-
cert '(Antient Concert :Rooms,: 4 p:m.),” whichwill be
the last of the season, is of a most varied and interest-
ing ‘nature. The-works allotted to the orchestra for
performance include the Overture to ‘‘ L’Espreuve
Villageoise,”” Grétry; Choral Prelude, ‘‘ Sleepers,
Awalke,” Bach; Scherzo from ‘° Midsummer Night’s
Dream,’”’ Mendelssohn; Quintet from ‘‘ Der Meister-
singers,”” Wagner; and Beethoven’s Symphony No. 8—
a truly great programme. Vocal items will be contri-
buted by Miss Queenie Eaton, and ‘Signor Iisposito, the
eminent pianist, and musical director of this:excellent
orchestra, will play two pianoforte solos, Chopin’s
‘“ Berceuse,”’ and Liszt’s Ballade. Tickets for this
magnificent concert should be obtained without delay
at Messrs. Pigott’s or Cramer’s.

Now many of our green people have been and are

“engaged in “‘ proving their tolerance '’ to the bigots,

and incidentally their intolerance to their own side; it
is partly done, or it is alleged that it is, with a view
_to_impressing such institutions as Belfast and the
. Irish Times.” But what Belfast and the “* Irish
Times ** are looking to are the facts and not the
ethics, and the fact that they see is that a lot of the
green Nationalists have not the pluck to sirike out in
self-defence.  So long as the fact facing the ‘‘ Irish
“Times >’ is that Nationalists are tame and won't bite,

A Northern correspondent writes:—** 1t is gratifying
to note that the United Trades and Labour Council of
Newry have passed a strongly worded resolution pro-
‘testing against the appointment of ‘a complete
stranger from England * to the captaincy of the local
fire brigade, while ¢ the Council had several local com-
petent men to clicose from,” and pointing out that the
Council should insert in future advertisements that ‘ no
Irish need apply.” How will the redoubtable Chair-
man, Mr. McCourt, take this knock-down blow? He,
it will be remembered, gave by his casting vote the
position to the Cockney. Maybe he will lend the fire
captain to Belfast when Churchill arrives.”’

Let Mr. Louis J. Walsh, Solicitor, of Ballycastle, a
well known Irish Irelander, one of the graduates of
University College in the days when ten years ago or
so University College, Dublin, produced young men of
national spirit and purpose—let Mr. Walsh give his
account of this latest act in the Devine Comedy :—

“Your readers have followed with such interest the
various disclosures in connection with the Degani case
that I think it right to inform them of the latest deve-
lopment. It is understood that the reason why the
University authoribies made such an undignified sur-
render to Miss Devine’s threat of a mandamus was be-
cause they had taken the opinion of one of the most
eminent K.C.’s at the Irish Bar, and were advised by
him that they had no justification whatever for refusing
Miss Devine her lectures. Miss Degani, however, ap-
pears to have kicked over the traces when she learned
of Miss Devine'’s admission; and in a letter dated the
22nd January from the College secrétary, Miss Devine
was informed that an application had been made by
Professor Degani to be relieved from the duty of deli-
vering lectures to Miss Devine owing to the latter’s
“ attitude ’ towards her, and Miss Devine was invited
to attend a special meeting of the Governing Body of
the College to be held on Saturday, the 27th January,
when the application would be considered. I attended
with Miss Devine at the meeting, and Miss Degani was
represented by Mr. Fitzgibbon, B.L. (instructed by Mr.
Martley, Solicitor).  Mr. Fitzgibbon stated that his
application was an extraordinary one; but that the cir-
cumstances justified it. Miss Degani’s nervous system
had been shattered by the agitation that had been car-
ried on about her appointment in ‘ certain newspapers.’
The attitude of Miss Devine towards her had not been
the attitude of a student towards a professor, and to
ask her to teach Miss Devine was like putting the de-
tective in the cell with his prisoner. Iivery slip that
the Professor made would be seized on by the student
to justify the report of Miss Degani’s alleged incompe-
tence that she had made. The Chief Baron and the
Attorney-General, who sat beside each other, seemed
to listen to counsel’s address rather impatiently. Both

papers and newspaper cuttings before them; and you
would have been proud had you seen how prominently
the LEADER figured in the bundle. ILvidently ! the
last of the Barons’ has been a diligent student of your
columns during the last few months. When Mr. Ititz-
gibbon had concluded, they both drew his attention to
the fact that he had not referred to certain letters of
which they were able to give the dates quite glibly.
Though they were sitting in a judicial capacity as mem-
bers of the Governing Body, they appeared to know
more about Mr. Fitzgibbon'’s case than he did himself !
Counsel fumbled through his brief for a few moments,
and then drew attention to the fact that these letters
were written by me in my private capacity, and that
Miss Devine could not be held responsible for my acts.
The Chief Baron and the Attorney-General looked dis-
appointed, and I felt a delightful feeling of pride and
exultation creeping over me, to think that the letters
from me, a poor, ignorant country solicitor, should be
treasured in the scrap books of eminent beings like the
Lord Chief Baron and the Attorney-General for Tre-
land! Tn reply to Dr. Coffey, I stated that my client
had no interest whatever in Miss Degani’s application.
The question whether that lady was going to be asked
to do any work in return for the salary which she drav
as Professor of Spanish was one of interest for the Trish
public; but it had no special interest for Miss Devine.
The University authorities were now under both a sta-
tutory and a contractual obligation to provide my client
with Spanish lectures, and they had so intimated to
_us: and naturally Miss Devine preferred lectures from
a competent lecturer. When I had thus delivered my-
self, the Attorney-General rose with the air of a man
who felt that he had now his victim in his grasp. He
settled his pile of newspapers and newspaper cuttings
before him and assumed the easy position of a counsel
who is going to conduct a triumphant cross-examina-
tion. ¢ Mr. Walsh,” he began slowly, whilst the Chief
Baron beamed encouragingly upon him, ‘ do you consi-
der that your letter of thé 25th of January was a proper
letter to write to the President of University College ?”
‘I am here to answer for Miss Devine,’ replied the
victim, ‘ not for Mr. Walsh’s conduct. The Governing
Body of University College have fortunately no jurisdic-
tion over me.” A look of blank disappointment spread
itself over the face of Mr. Attorney-General, and the
Chief Baron looked round apparently to order the police-

man to have the witness arrested for contempt of

court; but seeing only the mild-looking Dr. Coffey be-
fore him, he suddenly remembered that he was not on
the Bench. The parties then retived, and after what
seemed to be a long and stormy debate inside, we were
called in again and informed that the Governing Body
had acceded to Miss Degani’s application. I then
asked the President when Miss Devine’s Spanish lec-
tures would begin, and after considerable hesitation he
advised me to apply for information at fhe College.
Thus the curtain was rung down on that act of the
farce ; but the end is not yet! In conclusion T wish to
inform the Chief Baron and - the Attorney-General
through the columns of your paper (which T am glad to
see they read so diligently) that T know exactly what
their hand is, and I implore them to play the card
which I know they were contemplating when they went
into Saturday’s meeting.’’

This Devine comedy is one of the finest Anglo-Irish
comedies we haye ever read, and we are eagerly wait-
ing for more. The Chief Baron is one ‘of the most

amusing characters in the piece—after all there’s no
comedian like an old comedian.  But what endless
other comedies may spring from this piece: ‘ Nerves.
in the National '’ would be a pointed and alliterative -
title for the next variation of this Devine comedy.
Supposing we, who know something of economics, took
it into our head to apply for admission to the lectures
of the National Economist, Professor.T. M. Kettle!.
Why Kettle might boil over with ‘‘nerves,”” and fol-
lowing the precedent established in the Devine
comedy, the sage and weighty Governing Body would
dispense Kettle from lecturing to us! Or supposing
William O’Brien wanted lectures in Inglish Literature
from that great literary expert and member of the-
committee of the Yibs, Robert Donovan, B.A.? Or
if Professor John M. O’Sullivan, the noted historian,
took a fancy to learn logic and psychology from the-
Rev. Professor Shine! “But to return to the serious
aspect of this Degani case.  Has Professor Degani
any student of Spanish, and, if not, for what is she.
paid? One student who wanted to attend her lectures
gave the professor the ‘‘nerves,” and then—so far
as we know—there was none! No students, no salary,
would be a rather fair motto. If professors cannot
attract students, or if they want to dodge the student on

The record of the Catholic Truth Conference of 191%
—“The Catholic Truth- Annual ’—is just published..
The first article, illustrated, is devoted to the late
Cardinal Moran.  The inaugural address by Canon
Barry, which gave a very needed stimulus to the cam-
paign against dirty literature, is only briefly reported—
the report only runs to three pages—and that is a pitys-
for the lecture was a good, common-sense one, and
we would like to have had itin full. Very Rev. Andrew.
Murphy’s fine paper, on ‘‘ Some Errors Incidental to.
Democratic Government,’’ is published in the volume,.
as are also ‘‘ Social Responsibilities of Catholics,” by-
Sir H. Bellingham, and “ The Irish at Home and
Abroad—A Contrast,”” by Father P. Dowling, of
Sydney. The Annual is excellently turned out, and
the various pictures and portraits that it contains add:
to its interest.

Next Sunday, Father Patrick Power, S.J., will
preach at St. Francis Xavier’s, Upper Gardiner Street,
the annual charity sermon in aid of the Sacred Heart
Home, Drumcondra. TIn the evening another appeal
will be made in St. Columba’s, Drumcondra, by Father
Patrick J. Dunlea, C.C. The Sacred Heart Home has
been established to receive Catholic children, who are
saved from proselytism—and by all account the prose-
lytisers are very busy these days seeking poor Catholic
youngsters whom they may gather into their Birds’
Nests. At present over 200 children are in the Home
and over 1,500 children have been rescued. Owing
to inadequate funds it has to refuse many deserving
cases. Intending subscribers who cannot attend the
sermons may send their subscriptions to His Grace the
Archbishop, to the preachers, or direct to the President
of the Home, Miss O’Brien, 94 Lower Baggot Street,

agreeable surprise, for I had begun to fear that
this discussion was about to degenerate into a mere
controversy. I am, indeed, glad to find that * Piran

good work for the Irish-Treland cause amongst the
youth of the country.  Could I but convince myself
that I had any little effect in bringing him intq the
ranks of those who advocate Ivish games for Trish Col-
leges, I would feel that T had done a good day’s worlk,
and that all my ploughing had not been in vain.

Now that we are agreed on this point that Ivish
games should be the principal games mn all Trish Col-
leges, I will pass over all our side issues, for, indeed,
my aim has not been to prove the superior muscle-
building qualities of any game as compared with
another; rather my object was to point out that Irish
games have a great cffect in building up the moral
strength of our boys as Irish boys, and that English
games have the opposite effect.

I regard the latter half of ‘‘ Piran’s '’ article as a
clear and convincing blow for Irish games, and as
embracing the sentiments of every Irishman who be-
lieves in preference for- Irish games. ‘‘ Piran ™ asks
a very pertinent question, who, indeed, are the Chris-
tian Brothers, and from whom are they sprung? They
may or may not be of a noble stock, but even if such
were the case they should surely bear in mind that they
have Irish boys to train up, and not English.

I am not very conversant with the details of the
suppression of hurling in St. Patrick’s Place, but I
understand that the boys who endeavoured to start
the game there were dealt with in a very summary
manner, and that this dreadful monster—patriotism—
was strangled in its cradle. It is hard to believe that
any lads of spirit would submit to such treatment.
While all the other Colleges of the county have thrown
themseves into the fight for Irish games, the Christian
College stands alone in its isolated grandeur, a monu-
ment to the source whence all our Anglicisation is
sprung.

I believe many of the youngsters at St. Patrick’s
Place DO regard themselves as patricians, and would
never contaminate themselves with the touch of a
hurley, but the great majority of the students are
drawn from the country districts, and I hold that these
should be catered for as well as the ‘‘ respectable
city-bred youths. There would be some excuse if the
Brothers had given the games a fair trial, and then
dropped them for want of support from the boys. But
the reverse was the case, the boys wished to play them,
but: the Brothers banned them. There was a touch

of pathos in the last despairing struggle of the boys
before they relinquished the hope of mtroducing Trish
games. to the Christian College and capitulated to the
forces brought to bear upon them. They clubbed to-
gether more or less secretly, and, raising t‘he“ncces-
sary affiliation fee, entered under the name of Red-
and-Blacks.”” But even this sacrificé did not satiate
the animosity of, the authorities, and Ireland lost

THE.story of the Irish Protestants as a body is a

sordid and depressing tale, with hardly a vein of
‘relieving romance or even decent tragedy. ~ For over
three centuries, in a country where the original natives
were turned out wholly to give tliem the land, as in
the north, or made virtual bondsmen under them as in

the rest of Ireland, they have remained foreigners, their
only attachment to the country being to- its revenues;
they used their power with merciless selfishness and
cruelty, even with ferocity, depriving the natives of all
rights, and according them only such tolerance as fell
short of legalizing indiscriminate murder and highway
robbery in- times of peace ; education was prohibited—in-
some cases a capital offence—and to the fanaticism of
the so-called Puritan was added the strain of the em-
boldened sneak-thief, expressed in the legislation which
gave them the right to take over their neighbour's pro-
perty in certain despicable circumstances, and take the
most valuable horse of a Catholic for five pounds.  That
is all old, of course, and too painful in all truth even for
a Catholic to dwell upon for the sake of our common
humanity ; but the whole story is not old; Jimancipa-
tion did not end it, nor the abolition of tithes, nor Dis-
establishment, when the Protestant official church was
compelled to cease the exaction of all direct tribute
from the natives just forty-two years ago; the columns
of Tne LEaper have disclosed that the Ascendancy still
retains its grasping hand upon all the positions of public
emolument and private importance which it can control
by the secret conspiracy which has taken the place of
statutory privilege. The most repulsive pat of the
story, and its slow evolution under the influence of
human progress, is that none of those outrageous tyran-
nies upon a people, not one of the confiscatory exactions,
not one of the spécial privileges, which any party with
-a modicum of civilization much less a respect for human
rights—an approach to a Christian conception of the
dignity of mankind—would be deeply ashamed to sug-
gest much less to exercise, not one did they freely re-
linquish: all were wrung out, one by one, by dreary
years of wasting agitation by the natives, in the face of
fierce and threatening opposition by the Irish Protes-
tants, wrung out of the necessities or the shamed con-
sciences of Englishmen.

That is a small part of the whole truth, but it is
sufficient for the present. And now these same people,
if their spokesmen tell the truth, are the most discon-
tented of any section in Ireland, because it is proposed
to inaugurate an era of ‘‘ equal rights for all, special
privileges for none,”’ but their opposition is not
honestly avowed upon the threatened loss of privilege
and perquisite, but on the mendacious plea that the
natives are intolerant. Instead of welcoming this de-
layed opportunity to square themselves with their con-
sciences, their countrymen and mankind, and being
anxious to expiate as they may the infamy to which
they are heirs, these Protestants are clamouring to
retain what is still left of it. 2

I was much mterested in a passage in the recent
notable speech of Lord Pirrie in Belfast, in which he
partly gave expression to a thought that has often been
in my mind. I wondered whether those Irish Protes-
tants who oppose to-day the removal of the last
shackles, the progress of equal rights, would not, if
they could, restore the ancient regime, close all the
schools save their own, prohibit the sending of the
youth to foreign schools, limit all rights of citizenship,
including the franchise and public, worship, to them-
selves, and incidentally go into the nearest horse fair
with five-pound notes, and do it all in the name of
liberty! I believe the Orangemen would. We would
like in charity to believe and maintain that this spirit
of tyrdnny, greed and malice, nurtured upon-a founda-
tion of the grossest ignorance, is limited to the Orange
lodges; but our charity and our hopes get a rude shock
when we read of speeches by bishops in which the same

spirit appears, clothed in better English and more ;

polite terms—polite by this comparison only. We may

Irish hearths, where the very children drink in the
glories of the great past, and here we were exemplifying
“* the child is father of the man’’ in our keen
appreciation of the old bardic tales of our land. Yes;
this proud feeling will not be transient, inasmuch as
the way was shown for making these treasures our
very own, thus leading on to deeper studies of our
ancient and romantic lore. M.

CORRESPONDENCE.

CHARMING ‘ TAMI!” CLONMEL.
January 22nd, 1912.

Dear Sir,—Having read * Observer’s’” letter in the
LEADER, re the managership of the Clonmel gasworlks,
I regret, being a Clonmelman, that I am compelled by
facts to agree with him as far as the appointing of Irish-
men to positions of trust in this town is concerned.
The committee of the Clonmel Technical School is com-
posed almost entirely of Nationalists. The. chairman
is president of the local branch of the U, I. League;
Yet the three principal positions in the school are in the
hands of Inglishmen, i.c., those of headmaster, assist-
ant headmaster, and commercial teacher. Of course
the staff includes some Irishmen, but those only hold
minor positions which the Englishman, considering
himself of a superior race, would not think worth while
bothering himself to obtain. Paddy is only a slave,
and slaves cannot be allowed to hold prominent posi-
tions. Irishmen may not have the opportunity of qua-
lifying for the p on of headmaster of a technical
school, but surely our schools and colleges, or at least
our universities, must be able to turn out fully quali-
fied commercial teachers. But the Clonme] Teclinical
Committee must seek across the Channel for one fit to
teach in their Institute.

In ‘‘ Observer’s” letter I read that they were ad-
vanced Nationalists in Clonmel. I wonder what tust
have created that impression in his mind. I have lived
amongst them quite a long time, and I must say that,
from what I've always seen of them, my opinion is very,
different. _ i

We who believe in our country’s regeneration and
who would like to see our people self-reliant, had high
hopes when labour was organised in Clonmel a few
years ago. Then, led by our M.P., with the war-cry
of ““ Labour and Nationality,”” we combined to over-
throw the old corporation, the majority of whose mem-
bers were considered antagonistic to the true national
ideal. We succeeded in this, but there ended our suc-
cess. ““ Herself *’ came down from Dublin Gastle and,
whether accidentally or otherwise, left-some microbes
after her which entered into the systems of our cor-
porators. Ever since they have been suffering from a
bad attack of ‘‘Anglicisation’ much worse than was
ever apparent in the old corporation. Between begging
of their masters to send to Clonmel some of the soldiers
who were, they heard, being brought home from South
Aftica, and giving over all important positions, under
their control, to foreigners, they have crushed the hopes
of those who were foolish enough to ““ put a slave on
horseback.””

SHANE.
